title: Repaint or let fade
summary: A Kyoto temple painter and a British conservator disagree about an old fresco. One wants to repaint; the other wants to let it fade. Each has a tradition behind them.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: At a small temple north of Kyoto, a 17th-century painted ceiling is fading. The local painter, Mr Sakai, is the seventh generation of his family to maintain it. A British conservator from a London museum, Dr Helena Marsh, has been invited to advise. They meet under the ceiling. They disagree.

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: It is time to repaint. The dragon's blue is almost gone. My grandfather repainted it in 1962. His father did it in 1908. Before that, somebody did it in the 1860s. The pigments do not last more than two human lifetimes in this humidity. The painting is repainted; the painting is also continuous. That is how this ceiling has stayed alive for four hundred years.

body.speaker: Dr Marsh
body.text: I understand the tradition, and I respect it. But once you repaint, the surface you produce in 2024 is not the 1962 surface, and certainly not the 17th-century one. We will lose the evidence. The Kyoto ceiling that scholars cite in articles will, technically, be a 21st-century object.

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: It already is, in part. The 1962 surface covers most of the original. We have known this for sixty years. The temple has accepted this for sixty years. The ceiling is not a museum object. It is part of a building used by worshippers every morning. They do not visit to see brushstrokes from 1652. They visit because the dragon is there.

body.speaker: Dr Marsh
body.text: But the dragon is there partly because somebody, four centuries ago, painted it. If we keep refreshing the surface, his hand is gone. There is no trace. In London we would clean carefully, stabilise the pigments where possible, and accept the fading as honest history.

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: I have read your conservators' reports. The British Museum cleans Egyptian objects which were originally bright, and now they show only stone. That is one philosophy. It is not the only one. Here we believe the act of repainting is itself the tradition. To stop repainting would be to break the line, not to preserve it.

body.speaker: Dr Marsh
body.text: Then could we, at least, photograph it carefully first, in very high resolution, so that future generations have access to the 1962 layer before it goes under the new one?

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: Of course. We always do. My grandfather photographed it before he painted, with the camera he was allowed in 1962. We have the prints. We will photograph it more carefully this time. I am not against your records. I am against the idea that the records are the painting.

body.speaker: Dr Marsh
body.text: I see. And if a temple beside this one chose differently — chose, say, to stop repainting and let the dragon fade quietly — would you respect that?

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: Of course. Every temple decides for itself. Some have let their paintings fade. They are honest in their own way. I would not paint over their ceiling, and they would not stop me painting over mine. The mistake is to think that one of us is the conservator and the other is not. Both of us are. We are conserving different things.

body.speaker: Dr Marsh
body.text: That is a sentence I will probably steal.

body.speaker: Mr Sakai
body.text: Steal it. I borrowed it from my grandfather.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Mr Sakai will repaint the dragon in the spring. Dr Marsh has accepted an invitation to be present, with permission to photograph the entire process. She has also asked, separately, to be allowed to visit a different temple, three valleys away, where the painters chose, a century ago, to stop. She wants to write a comparative paper. Mr Sakai has offered to introduce her.

annotations.meaningVi: giới thiệu bà ấy (với người khác)

grammarNotes.title: Modal would for hypothetical and polite proposal
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'would' để diễn tả giả định hoặc đề nghị lịch sự. Trong bài: 'In London we would clean carefully, stabilise the pigments where possible' (giả định: nếu là chúng tôi, chúng tôi sẽ...). 'I would not paint over their ceiling, and they would not stop me' (giả định trái chiều). 'Could we, at least, photograph it carefully first' ('could' + đề nghị lịch sự). 'Would you respect that?' (lịch sự hỏi). So sánh với 'will' chỉ tương lai chắc chắn: 'Mr Sakai will repaint the dragon in the spring.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'would' for hypotheticals and polite proposals. In the text: 'In London we would clean carefully, stabilise the pigments where possible' (hypothetical: if it were us, we would...). 'I would not paint over their ceiling, and they would not stop me' (mutual hypothetical). 'Could we, at least, photograph it carefully first' ('could' + polite proposal). 'Would you respect that?' (polite question). Compare 'will' for confident future: 'Mr Sakai will repaint the dragon in the spring.'

translationVi:
Tại một ngôi chùa nhỏ phía bắc Kyoto, một trần được vẽ thế kỷ 17 đang phai. Người thợ vẽ địa phương, ông Sakai, là thế hệ thứ bảy trong gia đình duy trì nó. Một chuyên gia bảo tồn người Anh từ một bảo tàng ở London, tiến sĩ Helena Marsh, được mời sang tư vấn. Họ gặp nhau dưới trần. Họ không đồng ý với nhau.

Ông Sakai: Đến lúc vẽ lại. Màu xanh của con rồng gần như mất. Ông tôi vẽ lại năm 1962. Cha ông vẽ năm 1908. Trước đó, có người vẽ năm 1860 mấy. Chất màu không bền quá hai đời người trong độ ẩm này. Bức tranh được vẽ lại; bức tranh cũng là liên tục. Đó là cách trần này 'còn sống' bốn trăm năm.

TS Marsh: Tôi hiểu truyền thống, và tôi tôn trọng. Nhưng khi anh vẽ lại, bề mặt anh tạo năm 2024 không phải bề mặt 1962, và chắc chắn không phải bề mặt thế kỷ 17. Ta mất bằng chứng. Trần Kyoto mà các học giả trích dẫn trong bài viết, về kỹ thuật, sẽ là một vật thế kỷ 21.

Ông Sakai: Nó đã là rồi, phần nào. Bề mặt 1962 che hầu hết bản gốc. Ta biết điều đó sáu mươi năm. Chùa đã chấp nhận sáu mươi năm. Trần không phải vật bảo tàng. Nó là phần của một công trình mà người đến cầu mỗi sáng. Họ không tới để xem nét cọ năm 1652. Họ tới vì con rồng có ở đó.

TS Marsh: Nhưng con rồng có ở đó một phần vì bốn thế kỷ trước có người vẽ nó. Nếu ta cứ làm mới bề mặt, bàn tay ông ấy biến mất. Không còn dấu. Ở London chúng tôi sẽ làm sạch cẩn thận, cố định chất màu nơi có thể, và chấp nhận sự phai như lịch sử trung thực.

Ông Sakai: Tôi đã đọc báo cáo của các nhà bảo tồn của bà. Bảo tàng Anh làm sạch các vật Ai Cập từng có màu sáng, giờ chỉ còn đá. Đó là một triết lý. Không phải triết lý duy nhất. Ở đây chúng tôi tin việc vẽ lại chính là truyền thống. Ngừng vẽ lại sẽ là cắt dòng truyền, không phải bảo tồn.

TS Marsh: Vậy ít nhất chúng ta có thể chụp ảnh thật cẩn thận, ở độ phân giải rất cao, để các thế hệ sau có thể tiếp cận lớp 1962 trước khi nó nằm dưới lớp mới?

Ông Sakai: Tất nhiên. Chúng tôi luôn làm vậy. Ông tôi đã chụp ảnh trước khi vẽ, bằng máy ảnh được phép thời 1962. Chúng tôi có ảnh in. Lần này chúng tôi sẽ chụp kỹ hơn. Tôi không phản đối tài liệu của bà. Tôi phản đối ý nghĩ rằng tài liệu chính là bức tranh.

TS Marsh: Tôi hiểu. Và nếu một ngôi chùa bên cạnh chọn khác — chẳng hạn, chọn ngừng vẽ lại và để rồng phai lặng — anh có tôn trọng không?

Ông Sakai: Tất nhiên. Mỗi chùa tự quyết. Có chùa để tranh phai. Họ trung thực theo cách của họ. Tôi sẽ không vẽ chèn lên trần của họ, và họ sẽ không ngăn tôi vẽ chèn lên trần của tôi. Sai lầm là cho rằng một trong hai chúng tôi là người bảo tồn còn bên kia thì không. Cả hai đều là. Chúng tôi bảo tồn những thứ khác nhau.

TS Marsh: Đó là câu tôi chắc sẽ 'ăn cắp'.

Ông Sakai: 'Ăn' đi. Tôi đã mượn nó từ ông tôi.

Ông Sakai sẽ vẽ lại con rồng vào mùa xuân. TS Marsh đã nhận lời có mặt, với phép chụp toàn bộ quá trình. Bà cũng xin riêng được phép thăm một chùa khác, ba thung lũng đi xa, nơi các thợ vẽ chọn ngừng cách đây một thế kỷ. Bà muốn viết một bài so sánh. Ông Sakai đã ngỏ ý giới thiệu.

questions.prompt: Where and what is the disagreement about?
options: A 17th-century painted ceiling at a small temple north of Kyoto — whether to repaint a fading dragon
Mr Sakai, the 7th-generation local painter; Dr Helena Marsh, a British conservator

questions.prompt: Why does Mr Sakai want to repaint?
options: Pigments don't last more than two human lifetimes in that humidity; repainting is how the ceiling has stayed alive for 400 years
He wants to charge a fee

questions.prompt: What is Dr Marsh's main worry?
options: Once repainted, the surface is a 21st-century object; the 17th-century evidence is lost
The wrong colour will be used

questions.prompt: How does Sakai answer 'we will lose evidence'?
options: The 1962 layer already covers most of the original; the temple has accepted this for 60 years; the ceiling is not a museum object — it is part of a working temple

questions.prompt: How does Marsh describe the London approach?
options: Clean carefully, stabilise pigments where possible, accept fading as honest history

questions.prompt: What philosophy does Sakai counter with?
options: The act of repainting is itself the tradition; stopping would break the line, not preserve it
High-resolution photography of the 1962 layer before the new layer goes on

questions.prompt: What is Sakai's response to documentation?
options: Photos are fine; he is against the idea that the records are the painting

questions.prompt: What is the central reframe Sakai offers?
options: Both are conservators — they are conserving different things
Only Japanese painters can be conservators
questions.hint: Near the end of the dialogue.

options: Sakai repaints in spring with Marsh present photographing; Marsh will visit a non-repainting temple three valleys away to write a comparative paper, Sakai introducing her

Two opposed approaches to old art can both be called 'conservation' — they protect different things (matter vs. continuity) — and respecting that can let scholars and craftsmen work together rather than past each other.
Paintings should be left to die.
questions.hint: Look at Sakai's reframe and the closing.

cloze.template:
Narrator: At a small temple north of Kyoto, a 17th-century painted ceiling is {{b1}}. The local painter, Mr Sakai, is the seventh generation of his family to maintain it. A British conservator from a London museum, Dr Helena Marsh, has been invited to advise. They meet under the ceiling. They disagree.
Mr Sakai: It is time to repaint. The dragon's blue is almost gone. My grandfather repainted it in 1962. His father did it in 1908. Before that, somebody did it in the 1860s. The {{b2}} do not last more than two human lifetimes in this humidity. The painting is repainted; the painting is also continuous. That is how this ceiling has stayed alive for four hundred years.
Dr Marsh: I understand the tradition, and I respect it. But once you repaint, the surface you produce in 2024 is not the 1962 surface, and certainly not the 17th-century one. We will lose the {{b3}}. The Kyoto ceiling that scholars cite in articles will, technically, be a 21st-century object.
Mr Sakai: It already is, in part. The 1962 surface covers most of the original. We have known this for sixty years. The temple has accepted this for sixty years. The ceiling is not a {{b4}} object. It is part of a building used by worshippers every morning. They do not visit to see brushstrokes from 1652. They visit because the dragon is there.
Dr Marsh: But the dragon is there partly because somebody, four centuries ago, painted it. If we keep refreshing the surface, his hand is gone. There is no {{b5}}. In London we would clean carefully, stabilise the pigments where possible, and accept the fading as honest history.
Mr Sakai: I have read your conservators' reports. The British Museum cleans Egyptian objects which were originally bright, and now they show only stone. That is one {{b6}}. It is not the only one. Here we believe the act of repainting is itself the tradition. To stop repainting would be to break the {{b7}}, not to preserve it.
Dr Marsh: Then could we, at least, photograph it carefully first, in very high {{b8}}, so that future generations have access to the 1962 layer before it goes under the new one?
Mr Sakai: Of course. We always do. My grandfather photographed it before he painted, with the camera he was allowed in 1962. We have the prints. We will photograph it more carefully this time. I am not against your records. I am against the idea that the records are the painting.
Dr Marsh: I see. And if a temple beside this one chose differently — chose, say, to stop repainting and let the dragon fade quietly — would you respect that?
Mr Sakai: Of course. Every temple decides for itself. Some have let their paintings fade. They are honest in their own way. I would not paint over their ceiling, and they would not stop me painting over mine. The mistake is to think that one of us is the conservator and the other is not. Both of us are. We are conserving {{b9}} things.
Dr Marsh: That is a sentence I will probably steal.
Mr Sakai: Steal it. I borrowed it from my grandfather.
Narrator: Mr Sakai will repaint the dragon in the {{b10}}. Dr Marsh has accepted an invitation to be present, with permission to photograph the entire process. She has also asked, separately, to be allowed to visit a different temple, three valleys away, where the painters chose, a century ago, to stop. She wants to write a {{b11}} paper. Mr Sakai has offered to introduce her.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Sakai says 'we are conserving different things.' Think of an object or place you care about (a family recipe, an old photo, a building). If someone offered to 'preserve' it, what version would they be preserving — the matter, the appearance, or the practice — and which would you choose?
